original: i want to debug a python codebase. Give me a megaprompt
enhanced: I want to debug a Python codebase. Give me a megaprompt.

assumptions.text: The user is debugging a Python codebase (not mixed-language) and has access to the source code.
assumptions.label: UNKNOWN
assumptions.rationale: The prompt only states 'I want to debug a Python codebase' but does not specify whether other languages are involved or whether the source is available.

assumptions.text: The user has basic familiarity with Python and can run code, read logs, and use a terminal or IDE.
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: This is likely but not explicitly stated; if false, the debugging workflow would need to be more guided or tool-specific.

assumptions.text: Standard Python debugging tools (pdb/ipdb, logging, tracebacks, profilers) are available and acceptable to use.
assumptions.label: VERIFIED
assumptions.rationale: These are part of the Python standard library and common ecosystem; no external verification needed.

assumptions.text: The codebase can be run locally or in a controlled environment where instrumentation (logs, debuggers) is allowed.
assumptions.label: HYPOTHESIS
assumptions.rationale: If the code can only run in a locked-down production environment, debugging strategies would need to be more constrained and log-only.

failure_modes: Debugging without a clear reproduction case leads to chasing symptoms instead of root causes.
Over-instrumenting the codebase (too many logs/breakpoints) makes it hard to see the relevant signal.
Assuming the bug is in the wrong layer (e.g., blaming application code when it is a misconfigured dependency or environment issue).
Fixing symptoms instead of the root cause, causing the bug to reappear in a slightly different form.
Not adding tests or regression checks after the fix, allowing the same bug to be reintroduced later.

    # ` — The Python Debugger
The module ` defines an interactive source code debugger for Python
programs.  It supports setting (conditional) breakpoints and single stepping at
the source line level, inspection of stack frames, source code listing, and
evaluation of arbitrary Python code in the context of any stack frame.  It also
supports post-mortem debugging and can be called under program control.

`).
This is currently undocumented but easily understood by reading the source.  The
extension interface uses the modules `) and `).

Module `)Used to dump Python tracebacks explicitly, on a fault, after a timeout,
or on a user signal.

Module `)Standard interface to extract, format and print stack traces of Python programs.

The typical usage to break into the debugger is to insert:

at the location you want to break into the debugger, and then run the program.
You can then step through the code following this statement, and continue
running without the debugger using the `) command.

The built-in `), when called with defaults, can be used
instead of   `.

   
      
  

```

The debugger’s prompt is `, which is the indicator that you are in debug mode:

 
 
  

 
    

```

Tab-completion via the `) module is available for commands and
command arguments, e.g. the current global and local names are offered as
arguments of the ` command.

## Command-line interface` from the command line to debug other scripts.  For
example:

             

```

When invoked as a module, pdb will automatically enter post-mortem debugging if
the program being debugged exits abnormally.  After post-mortem debugging (or
after normal exit of the program), pdb will restart the program.  Automatic
restarting preserves pdb’s state (such as breakpoints) and in most cases is more
useful than quitting the debugger upon program’s exit.

` option with the target process’s PID:

    

```

Typical usage to execute a statement under control of the debugger is:

      

```

The typical usage to inspect a crashed program is:
```

The *commands* argument, if given, is a list of commands to execute
when the debugger starts.

The keyword-only argument *header*.

```
The *skip* argument, if given, must be an iterable of glob-style module name
```

square brackets (`) in the command syntax; the square brackets must not be

Entering a blank line repeats the last command entered.  Exception: if the last
command was a `) command, the next 11 lines are listed.

Commands that the debugger doesn’t recognize are assumed to be Python statements
and are executed in the context of the program being debugged.  Python
statements can also be prefixed with an exclamation point (`).  This is a
powerful way to inspect the program being debugged; it is even possible to
change a variable or call a function.  When an exception occurs in such a
statement, the exception name is printed but the debugger’s state is not
changed.

Expressions/Statements whose prefix is a pdb command are now correctly
identified and executed.

The debugger supports ).  Aliases can have
parameters which allows one a certain level of adaptability to the context under
examination.

Multiple commands may be entered on a single line, separated by `.  (A
single ` is not used as it is the separator for multiple commands in a line
that is passed to the Python parser.)  No intelligence is applied to separating
the commands; the input is split at the first ` pair, even if it is in the
middle of a quoted string. A workaround for strings with double semicolons
is to use implicit string concatenation ` or `.

To set a temporary global variable, use a *convenience variable*. A *convenience

web_discovery_results.deep_title: pdb — The Python Debugger — Python 3.14.4 documentation
web_discovery_results.summary: The page documents the Python standard debugger, `pdb`, and how to use it to debug Python codebases. It covers both interactive debugging (setting breakpoints, stepping through code, inspecting frames) and programmatic entry points (`pdb.set_trace()`, `pdb.run()`, `pdb.runeval()`, `pdb.runcall()`, `pm()`, `post_mortem()`). It also explains command-line usage (`python -m pdb`), attaching to running processes, and advanced configuration of the `Pdb` class (skip patterns, signal handling, colorization, backend selection). This is directly relevant to debugging a Python codebase.
key_facts: `pdb` is the standard interactive source-level debugger for Python, supporting breakpoints, single-stepping, stack frame inspection, source listing, and arbitrary code evaluation in any frame.
The simplest way to break into the debugger is to insert `import pdb; pdb.set_trace()` (or `breakpoint()`) at the desired location in your code and run the program.

`pdb` can be invoked from the command line with `python -m pdb <script.py>` to debug a script, including options for post-mortem debugging, module execution, and attaching to a running process by PID.
Programmatic entry points include `pdb.run(statement)`, `pdb.runeval(expression)`, `pdb.runcall(function, ...)`, `pdb.post_mortem(traceback_or_exception)`, and `pdb.pm()` for post-mortem debugging.

The `Pdb` class can be customized with `skip` patterns to avoid stepping into certain modules, `nosigint` to control SIGINT handling, `readrc` to control `.pdbrc` loading, `mode` for invocation-specific behavior, `backend` selection (`readline` vs `prompt_toolkit`), and `colorize` for colorized source output.
Tab completion is available in the debugger prompt via the `readline` module, and the debugger prompt is `(Pdb)`.
Post-mortem debugging can be entered automatically when using `python -m pdb` if the program exits abnormally, and the debugger can be configured to automatically restart the program afterward.
relevant_quotes: The module `pdb` defines an interactive source code debugger for Python programs. It supports setting (conditional) breakpoints and single stepping at the source line level, inspection of stack frames, source code listing, and evaluation of arbitrary Python code in the context of any stack frame.
The typical usage to break into the debugger is to insert: `import pdb; pdb.set_trace()` at the location you want to break into the debugger, and then run the program.
The built-in `breakpoint()`, when called with defaults, can be used instead of `import pdb; pdb.set_trace()`.
When invoked as a module, pdb will automatically enter post-mortem debugging if the program being debugged exits abnormally.
The debugger’s prompt is `(Pdb)`, which is the indicator that you are in debug mode.
Tab-completion via the `readline` module is available for commands and command arguments, e.g. the current global and local names are offered as arguments of the `p` command.
To attach to a running Python process for remote debugging, use the `--pid` or `--attach-pid` option with the target process’s PID.

    # ` — Logging facility for Python)

- )

- )

This module defines functions and classes which implement a flexible event
logging system for applications and libraries.

The key benefit of having the logging API provided by a standard library module
is that all Python modules can participate in logging, so your application log
can include your own messages integrated with messages from third-party
modules.

Here’s a simple example of idiomatic usage:

  

     
    
    
    

   
    

```

  

    

```

If you run *myapp.py*, you should see this in *myapp.log*:

INFO:__main__:Started
INFO:mylib:Doing something
INFO:__main__:Finished

```

The key feature of this idiomatic usage is that the majority of code is simply
creating a module level logger with `, and using that
logger to do any needed logging. This is concise, while allowing downstream
code fine-grained control if needed. Logged messages to the module-level logger
get forwarded to handlers of loggers in higher-level modules, all the way up to
the highest-level logger known as the root logger; this approach is known as
hierarchical logging.

For logging to be useful, it needs to be configured: setting the levels and
destinations for each logger, potentially changing how specific modules log,
often based on command-line arguments or application configuration. In most
cases, like the one above, only the root logger needs to be so configured, since
all the lower level loggers at module level eventually forward their messages to
its handlers.  `) provides a quick way to configure
the root logger that handles many use cases.

The module provides a lot of functionality and flexibility.  If you are
unfamiliar with logging, the best way to get to grips with it is to view the
tutorials (**see the links above and on the right**).

The basic classes defined by the module, together with their attributes and
methods, are listed in the sections below.

## Logger Objects`.  Multiple calls to `) with the same
name will always return a reference to the same Logger object.

The ` is potentially a period-separated hierarchical value, like
` (though it could also be just plain `, for example).
Loggers that are further down in the hierarchical list are children of loggers
higher up in the list.  For example, given a logger with a name of `,
loggers with names of `, `, and ` are all
descendants of `.  In addition, all loggers are descendants of the root
logger. The logger name hierarchy is analogous to the Python package hierarchy,
and identical to it if you organise your loggers on a per-module basis using
the recommended construction `.  That’s because
in a module, ` is the module’s name in the Python package namespace.

*

This is the logger’s name, and is the value that was passed to `)
to obtain the logger.

The threshold of this logger, as set by the `) method.

`),
which has checks for the level passed to it.

The parent logger of this logger. It may change based on later instantiation
of loggers which are higher up in the namespace hierarchy.

If this attribute evaluates to true, events logged to this logger will be
passed to the handlers of higher level (ancestor) loggers, in addition to
any handlers attached to this logger. Messages are passed directly to the
ancestor loggers’ handlers - neither the level nor filters of the ancestor
loggers in question are considered.

If this evaluates to false, logging messages are not passed to the handlers
of ancestor loggers.

Spelling it out with an example: If the propagate attribute of the logger named
` evaluates to true, any event logged to ` via a method call such as
` will [subject to passing that logger’s
level and filter settings] be passed in turn to any handlers attached to loggers
named `, ` and the root logger, after first being passed to any handlers
attached to `. If any logger in the chain `, `, ` has its
` attribute set to false, then that is the last logger whose handlers
are offered the event to handle, and propagation stops at that point.

The constructor sets this attribute to `.

`. A common
scenario is to attach handlers only to the root logger, and to let
propagation take care of the rest.

The list of handlers directly attached to this logger instance.

`) and `) methods, which use locks to ensure
thread-safe operation.

This attribute disables handling of any events. It is set to ` in the
initializer, and only changed by logging configuration code.

*
Sets the threshold for this logger to *level*. Logging messages which are less
severe than *level* will be ignored; logging messages which have severity *level*
or higher will be emitted by whichever handler or handlers service this logger,
unless a handler’s level has been set to a higher severity level than *level*.

When a logger is created, the level is set to `) (which causes
all messages to be processed when the logger is the root logger, or delegation
to the parent when the logger is a non-root logger). Note that the root logger
is created with level `).

The term ‘delegation to the parent’ means that if a logger has a level of
NOTSET, its chain of ancestor loggers is traversed until either an ancestor with
a level other than NOTSET is found, or the root is reached.

If an ancestor is found with a level other than NOTSET, then that ancestor’s
level is treated as the effective level of the logger where the ancestor search
began, and is used to determine how a logging event is handled.

If the root is reached, and it has a level of NOTSET, then all messages will be
processed. Otherwise, the root’s level will be used as the effective level.

See ) for a list of levels.

The *level* parameter now accepts a string representation of the
level such as ‘INFO’ as an alternative to the integer constants
such as `). Note, however, that levels are internally stored
as integers, and methods such as e.g. `) and
`) will return/expect to be passed integers.

*
Indicates if a message of severity *level* would be processed by this logger.
This method checks first the module-level level set by
` and then the logger’s effective level as determined
by `).

Indicates the effective level for this logger. If a value other than
`) has been set using `), it is returned. Otherwise,
the hierarchy is traversed towards the root until a value other than
` is found, and that value is returned. The value returned is
an integer, typically one of `), `)
etc.

*
Returns a logger which is a descendant to this logger, as determined by the suffix.
Thus, ` would return the same
logger as would be returned by `. This is a
convenience method, useful when the parent logger is named using e.g. `
rather than a literal string.

Returns a set of loggers which are immediate children of this logger. So for
example ` might return a set containing
loggers named ` and `, but a logger named ` wouldn’t be
included in the set. Likewise, ` might
return a set including a logger named `, but it wouldn’t include one
named `.

*, *, *
Logs a message with level `) on this logger. The *msg* is the
message format string, and the *args* are the arguments which are merged into
*msg* using the string formatting operator. (Note that this means that you can
use keywords in the format string, together with a single dictionary argument.)
No % formatting operation is performed on *msg* when no *args* are supplied.

There are four keyword arguments in *kwargs* which are inspected:
*exc_info*, *stack_info*, *stacklevel* and *extra*.

If *exc_info* does not evaluate as false, it causes exception information to be
added to the logging message. If an exception tuple (in the format returned by
`)) or an exception instance is provided, it is used;
otherwise, ` is called to get the exception information.

The second optional keyword argument is *stack_info*, which defaults to
`. If true, stack information is added to the logging
message, including the actual logging call. Note that this is not the same
stack information as that displayed through specifying *exc_info*: The
former is stack frames from the bottom of the stack up to the logging call
in the current thread, whereas the latter is information about stack frames
which have been unwound, following an exception, while searching for
exception handlers.

You can specify *stack_info* independently of *exc_info*, e.g. to just show
how you got to a certain point in your code, even when no exceptions were
raised. The stack frames are printed following a header line which says:

Stack (most recent call last):

```

This mimics the     ` which is used when
displaying exception frames.

The third optional keyword argument is *stacklevel*, which defaults to `.

web_discovery_results.deep_title: logging — Logging facility for Python — Python 3.14.4 documentation
web_discovery_results.summary: The Python logging module provides a flexible event logging system that is especially useful for debugging a Python codebase. It supports hierarchical loggers (typically one per module via `logging.getLogger(__name__)`), configurable logging levels, and handlers that control where log messages go (e.g., files, console). The idiomatic pattern is to create a module-level logger and emit messages at different severity levels (DEBUG, INFO, WARNING, ERROR, CRITICAL), then configure the root logger (e.g., with `logging.basicConfig`) to control which messages are shown and where they are written. Loggers propagate messages up to ancestor loggers by default, which allows you to attach handlers only to the root logger and still capture messages from all modules. This makes it easy to add structured, level-based logging throughout your codebase for debugging and monitoring.
key_facts: The logging module defines functions and classes for a flexible event logging system used by applications and libraries.
The idiomatic usage is to create a module-level logger with `logging.getLogger(__name__)` and use that logger to emit messages.
Loggers are organized hierarchically by name (period-separated, analogous to Python package names), and all loggers are descendants of the root logger.
Messages logged to a module-level logger are forwarded to handlers of ancestor loggers, up to the root logger, enabling centralized logging configuration.
Logging must be configured (levels and destinations) to be useful; in many cases only the root logger needs configuration.
`logging.basicConfig()` provides a quick way to configure the root logger for common use cases.
Logger levels determine which messages are processed; messages below the set level are ignored.
Loggers have a `propagate` attribute; if true, events are passed to ancestor loggers' handlers in addition to the logger's own handlers.
The module supports multiple handlers, allowing log messages to be sent to different outputs such as files and the console.
The documentation recommends viewing the tutorials linked from the logging page for users unfamiliar with logging.
relevant_quotes: This module defines functions and classes which implement a flexible event logging system for applications and libraries.

The key benefit of having the logging API provided by a standard library module is that all Python modules can participate in logging, so your application log can include your own messages integrated with messages from third-party modules.
The key feature of this idiomatic usage is that the majority of code is simply creating a module level logger with `logging.getLogger(__name__)`, and using that logger to do any needed logging.

Logged messages to the module-level logger get forwarded to handlers of loggers in higher-level modules, all the way up to the highest-level logger known as the root logger; this approach is known as hierarchical logging.

For logging to be useful, it needs to be configured: setting the levels and destinations for each logger, potentially changing how specific modules log, often based on command-line arguments or application configuration.
In most cases, like the one above, only the root logger needs to be so configured, since all the lower level loggers at module level eventually forward their messages to its handlers.
`logging.basicConfig()` provides a quick way to configure the root logger that handles many use cases.

web_discovery_results.title: pdb — The Python Debugger — Python 3.12.13 documentation
web_discovery_results.content: Mar 12, 2013 · The module pdb defines an interactive source code debugger for Python programs. It supports setting (conditional) breakpoints and single stepping at the source line level, inspection of stack frames, source code listing, and evaluation of arbitrary Python code in the context of any stack frame.

New in version 3.7: The built-in breakpoint(), when called with defaults, can be used instead of import pdb; pdb.set_trace(). The typical usage to inspect a crashed program is: >>> import pdb >>> import mymodule >>> mymodule.test() Traceback (most recent call last): File "<stdin>", line 1, in <module> File "./mymodule.py", line 4, in test test2() File "./mymodule.py"
